findings.title: at(), every(), cron() methods share near-identical structure
findings.description: The three scheduling methods at(), every(), and cron() follow the exact same pattern: generate id with nanoid(), build record via buildRecord(), save to store, schedule the job, return id. The only variation is a one-line transformation of the first argument into a schedule string. This is a near-duplicate block repeated 3 times (7 lines each, 21 lines total).

findings.title: Switch in scheduleJob() could use a mode-to-config lookup
findings.code_snippet:
switch (record.mode) {
  case 'at':
    cronJob = new Cron(new Date(record.schedule), cronOptions, callback)
    break
  case 'every':
    cronJob = new Cron('* * * * * *', { ...cronOptions, interval: Number(record.schedule) }, callback)
    break
  case 'cron':
    cronJob = new Cron(record.schedule, cronOptions, callback)
    break
}
findings.description: The switch statement maps each mode to slightly different Cron constructor arguments. This is not severely complex (cyclomatic +3), but a small helper that produces {pattern, options} per mode would make the intent clearer and keep scheduleJob() focused on wiring rather than mode dispatch.

findings.title: save() uses 11 positional SQL parameters, hard to audit
findings.code_snippet:
this.db.prepare(`
  INSERT OR REPLACE INTO cron_jobs (id, name, mode, schedule, handler, payload, timezone, enabled, created_at, last_run_at, run_count)
  VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
`).run(
  job.id, job.name, job.mode, job.schedule, job.handler,
  job.payload, job.timezone, job.enabled ? 1 : 0,
  job.createdAt, job.lastRunAt, job.runCount,
)
findings.description: The INSERT statement uses 11 positional placeholders (?) and 11 positional arguments. Verifying that each ? maps to the correct column requires counting columns and arguments in parallel. better-sqlite3 supports named parameters ($name, @name, or :name) which make the mapping self-documenting.
findings.suggestion:
BEFORE:
this.db.prepare(`
  INSERT OR REPLACE INTO cron_jobs (id, name, mode, ...)
  VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
`).run(job.id, job.name, job.mode, ...)

AFTER:
this.db.prepare(`
  INSERT OR REPLACE INTO cron_jobs (id, name, mode, schedule, handler, payload, timezone, enabled, created_at, last_run_at, run_count)
  VALUES ($id, $name, $mode, $schedule, $handler, $payload, $timezone, $enabled, $created_at, $last_run_at, $run_count)
`).run({
  id: job.id,
  name: job.name,
  mode: job.mode,
  schedule: job.schedule,
  handler: job.handler,
  payload: job.payload,
  timezone: job.timezone,
  enabled: job.enabled ? 1 : 0,
  created_at: job.createdAt,
  last_run_at: job.lastRunAt,
  run_count: job.runCount,
})

WHY: Named parameters make the column-to-value mapping self-documenting. Eliminates the risk of column/value ordering mismatch as the schema grows. No runtime cost -- better-sqlite3 handles named params natively.

positive_observations: Both files are well under the 200-line guideline with clean, focused methods
JobStore correctly uses parameterized queries (?) throughout -- no SQL injection risk
Guard clauses and early returns used consistently (scheduleJob, start, pause, resume)
Pure mapRow function at module scope follows functional core pattern for data mapping
Constructor error handling in JobStore properly closes DB on init failure before re-throwing
CronService.close() composes stop() and store.close() cleanly -- no resource leak paths
Types are well-defined in a separate cron-types.ts with ScheduleMode union type
